title: Sawhorse Outdoor Table
summary: An outdoor dining table on two angled sawhorse-style legs, with a compound-angle center cross support — a distinct base system from the trestle and pedestal tables elsewhere here.
description:
This table's base is two real sawhorses — angled 2x6 legs splayed for stability — rather than a trestle or pedestal, with the tabletop simply laid across them and screwed down from underneath. Build each sawhorse leg set with angled bevel cuts, attaching the legs to the base supports with pocket holes and glue, then repeat for the second set.

The angled stretchers between each sawhorse's legs come next, followed by a straight center stretcher spanning between the two angled leg sets, centered carefully since it's what keeps the whole base rigid lengthwise. **Lay the tabletop boards out flat before attaching the sawhorses to them** — screwing the base to an already-fussy, unaligned set of boards is much harder than aligning the boards first and dropping the base on top.

The cross supports underneath use two different bevel angles — 40° and 50° — though **the plan offers a genuine fallback if your saw doesn't cut a 50° bevel**: use 45° on both cuts instead and simply adjust the cross support's position on the stretcher to compensate. An optional 2x4 frame around the outside edge of the tabletop, added last, gives the whole piece a more finished, framed look.

materials.name: Primer and paint, or exterior stain

cutList.note: Long point to short point, both ends cut 10° bevel.
Long point to long point, both ends cut 45° bevel, ends not parallel.
Long point to long point, both ends cut 10° off square, ends not parallel.
Both ends beveled, one at 40° and one at 50° (or both at 45° as a fallback).
Cut to fit around the tabletop.

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body: From the 2x6: four tabletop boards 88" (one per 8 ft board), eight sawhorse legs 27-3/8" measured long point to short point with both ends beveled 10°, four short base supports 7-7/8" long point to long point with both ends cut 10° off square and not parallel, and two straight base supports 26". The legs and supports pack into four more 8 ft boards, so eight of the nine 2x6s on the list are consumed — keep the ninth, because a later step needs a stretcher the cut list never sizes. From the 2x4: three angled stretchers 39-1/2" long point to long point, both ends beveled 45° and not parallel, and five cross supports 34" with one end beveled 40° and the other 50". If your saw will not reach 50°, cut both ends of the cross supports at 45° instead and shift each one along its stretcher to make up the difference — the plan offers that fallback deliberately. Set the saw once per angle and cut all the matching pieces in that one setting: eight legs that are not identical will not stand level, and no amount of shimming afterwards fixes it.

steps.title: Build the sawhorse leg sets
steps.body: Each sawhorse is one 26" straight base support with a 7-7/8" short support at each end and two legs splaying off it. Drill 1-1/2" pocket holes in the top end of every leg, then fasten the legs to the supports with 2-1/2" pocket hole screws and glue, letting the 10° bevel at the top of each leg sit flat against the underside of the support so the leg leans outward rather than standing plumb. Work on a flat surface. Build both sawhorses, then set them side by side and measure the spread between the feet on each — if the two differ, the table will rock on whichever pair is narrower no matter how flat the top is.

steps.title: Attach the angled stretchers
steps.body: Fit an angled stretcher between the two splayed legs of each sawhorse, so its 45° bevels land flat against the inside faces of the legs. Hold it in position, mark both legs, then fasten with countersunk 2-1/2" self-tapping screws or 2-1/2" pocket hole screws driven from the back face where they will not show. Set both stretchers at the same height off the floor on the two sawhorses. Note that the cut list gives three 39-1/2" stretchers for two sawhorses — use two here and set the third aside; where the third one goes is not settled by the plan's own dimensions, so check the photo before you fit it.

steps.title: Attach the straight stretcher
steps.body: This is the one part the cut list does not size. The plan calls for a straight stretcher running the length of the table between the two sawhorse assemblies, but every straight 2x6 row is already spoken for — the two 26" base supports are one per sawhorse. Stand the two sawhorses under the 88" tabletop at the spacing you want, far enough in from the ends that nobody kicks a leg and close enough together that the top does not sag between them, measure the actual gap, and cut a stretcher to that length from the spare 2x6. Centre it on both base supports and fasten with countersunk 2-1/2" self-tapping screws or pocket holes. This piece is what stops the base folding lengthwise, so do not skip it because the cut list forgot it.

steps.title: Lay out and attach the tabletop
steps.body: Lay the four 88" tabletop boards flat and edge to edge, best faces up — four 5-1/2" boards make a top 22" wide by 88" long. Shuffle them for grain and colour, push them tight, and pull the ends into line so both ends of the top are square. Now set the sawhorse base upside down on the underside, inset from each end, and drive 2-1/2" self-tapping screws up through the base supports into the boards — at least two screws into every board, so no board can cup or twist on its own. Aligning the boards first and dropping the base onto them is far easier than trying to straighten boards under a base that is already fixed, which is why the plan is explicit about this order.

steps.title: Add the middle base support
steps.body: Add the middle base support with glue and 2-1/2" screws, centred on the run between the two sawhorses. The plan gives this part no cut-list row of its own, so take its length off the assembly in front of you rather than from a number — and if you already cut a centre stretcher to fit in the previous step, check the photo before adding a second piece, because the plan's data does not make clear whether these are two parts or one described twice.

steps.title: Add the cross supports
steps.body: Fasten the 34" cross supports underneath, each one running from the stretcher up to the underside of the tabletop, with its 40° bevel landing flat at one end and its 50° bevel at the other. Predrill before driving the 2-1/2" self-tapping screws — a bevel-cut end is short grain and splits readily. If you used the 45°/45° fallback because your saw would not reach 50°, slide each support along the stretcher until both beveled faces sit flat against their mating surfaces before you fasten it; with the fallback, position is what makes up for the angle you could not cut.

steps.title: Add the optional table frame
steps.body: If you want the framed look, wrap a 2x4 frame around the outside edge of the 22" x 88" top, mitering the four corners, and fasten it with 2-1/2" screws driven from underneath so nothing shows on the finished surface. Cut to fit rather than to the cut list's 88": mitered around the top, the two long sides come out about 91" and the two ends about 25", and that does come out of the three 8 ft boards listed. Four literal 88" pieces would not — two 88" lengths will not share a 96" board, so that reading needs four boards, not three. Cut the two long pieces first, fit them, then measure the ends off the assembly.

steps.title: Finish
steps.body: Sand every surface, and take the time to ease the top edges and the corners of the tabletop and the leg bottoms — this is an outdoor table people will lean on with bare forearms. Then prime and paint, or use an exterior stain. Whichever you choose, get finish onto the end grain of all four tabletop boards and the bottoms of the eight legs before the table goes outside: end grain drinks water, and the leg bottoms sit in whatever puddles on the patio.
materials: Primer and paint, or exterior stain

images.alt: Outdoor dining table on angled sawhorse legs
